titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Ecclesia Ministry Records
titlestmt.author: Sarah Graves

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Wednesday November 27, 2024 at 18:16

did.abstract: This collection documents Ecclesia Ministries, an inner-city outreach ministry begun in 1994, and the spiritual formation and writings of Ecclesia founder Rev. Deborah Little Wyman, whose vision for "street ministry" grew out of her desire to minister to the unhoused community in the Back Bay neighborhood of Boston. Papers, photographs, and sermons provide insight into weekly Sunday worship beginning in 1996 with an outdoor worship service at the fountain at the Boston Common. The unhoused community that gathered came to refer to their weekly outdoor church space and community as "common cathedral."

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Ecclesia Ministries Records (RG 332), Special Collections, Yale Divinity Library.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Deborah Little Wyman received an M.Div. from General Theological Seminary in New York in May 1994, was ordained as a deacon in the Episcopal Church on June 4, 1994, at the Cathedral Church of St. Paul in Boston, Massachusetts, and a priest on October 14, 1995, at Emmanuel Church in Boston. Rev. Little Wyman's exposure to the writings of Central and South American liberation theologies in the early 1980s was formative to discovering her call to the priesthood and the conception of Ecclesia Ministries. Ecclesia Ministries was founded on Little Wyman's vision of Jesus' life of ministering to the poor and her desire to engage in what she called "street ministry," which she would ultimately imagine existing outside of the traditional four walls of a church building.

Unofficially, Ecclesia Ministries began following Rev. Little Wyman's ordination as a deacon, when she began to go out onto the streets daily with a backpack of sandwiches and supplies, with the intention of building relationships. A small group gathered for Holy Communion at the Boston Common on Easter Day in 1996, inaugurating the first of many consecutive Sundays of worship followed by a simple meal and fellowship. The growing community included unhoused and housed folks, staff, volunteers, and interns to support Ecclesia's emerging ministry.

Common cathedral, named as such by its community, was pivotal to the organization that would become Ecclesia Ministries. Radical hospitality was central to Ecclesia's street ministry, which included pastoral care, teaching and study, community engagement, and outdoor worship services and other programming throughout the week. Liturgy was adapted from the Book of Common Prayer of the Episcopal Church, and Holy Communion was celebrated weekly. In 2004, Ecclesia Ministries received additional funding, enabling "Come and See," providing programming and support for affiliate ministries.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: These papers offer an overview of the founding and implementation of a vibrant, ecumenical organization supporting and uplifting the lives of unhoused people through sacramentally-focused ministry and relationships. The collection is divided into two series; Series I, Ecclesia Ministries, which documents the creation and work of Ecclesia Ministries, and Series II, Deborah Little Wyman, which includes papers relating to her spiritual formation and work in ordained ministry.

Series I, Ecclesia Ministries, subseries Administrative Records, traces the mission and theological framework in the organization's founding and beyond. This subseries additionally includes financial and fundraising records, correspondence, and other foundational particularities, such as information on the origins of the altar and cross used at common cathedral. Series I, Ecclesia Ministries, subseries Staff, Volunteer and Attendee Records, documents the faces of common cathedral, including written reflections and testimonies from participants, highlighting the centrality of community building to the organization. Series I, Ecclesia Ministries, subseries Affiliate Program, documents Ecclesia's witness and expansion beyond Boston to a variety of additional localized sites. This includes affiliate directories, correspondence, training materials, and media coverage. Series I, Ecclesia Ministries, subseries Program Documentation, contains liturgical materials from common cathedral including bulletins, sermons, and sermon notes from the 1990s to 2010s organized according to the liturgical year, due to an effort to preserve the liturgical centrality of the organization and its founder. This series also documents "Come and See," which facilitated the development and training of street ministries in additional cities. Series I, Ecclesia Ministries, subseries Publications and Publicity, includes newsletters and promotional materials generated by Ecclesia. Series I, Ecclesia Ministries, subseries Collected Materials on Ecclesia, contains magazine and newspaper articles written about the organization's work. The bulk of media coverage on Ecclesia was collected and organized chronologically in a series of bound portfolios; see "Ecclesia Clippings" Volumes 1-6, spanning the years 1994-2010. Series I, Ecclesia Ministries, subseries Audiovisual Materials, includes photographs of common cathedral worship services and fellowship taken by staff and participants, in addition to audio and visual recordings documenting program.

Series II, Deborah Little Wyman, focuses on her discernment, spiritual formation, ordination, published writings, and personal unpublished writings that impacted the creation of Ecclesia Ministries. While Rev. Little Wyman's vocation is indistinguishable at times from the organization she founded, this series contains sermons, writings, and public speaking opportunities occurring beyond her work at common cathedral, though often witnessing to the work of Ecclesia Ministries. Series II, Deborah Little Wyman, subseries Biographical Documentation, includes administrative records on Rev. Little Wyman's ordination in the Episcopal Church and materials relating to her writing and vocation. Series II, Deborah Little Wyman, subseries Writings, begins with early writings in the form of journal entries and focuses on personal history and goals, spiritual formation, supervised ministry fieldwork, and reflections on her emerging life in ministry. Other writings include final papers and theses for her MDiv and DMin degrees, such as her DMin thesis Church Under the Tree, a collection of oral histories narrating her experiences in street ministry. The bulk of the materials in this subseries are sermons preached by Rev. Little Wyman from 1989-2013, occasionally accompanied by a service bulletin, after which follow other public speaking engagements spanning from 1995-2014, and liturgies and planning notes for weddings, funerals, memorials, baptisms, and ordinations not connected to common cathedral. Finally, copies of Rev. Little Wyman's published works are included; her book Home Care for the Dying (1985), a chapter titled "Theology of the Poor" in Handbook of U.S. Theologies of Liberation titled (2004), and various published articles from 1998-2000. Series II, Deborah Little Wyman, subseries Correspondence, notably includes correspondence with David E. Johnson, former bishop of the Episcopal Diocese of Massachusetts, and expresses her disappointment with the church's decision not to take a stance on the issue of blessing same-sex marriages. Series II, Deborah Little Wyman, subseries Collected Materials is largely comprised of sermons, liturgies, and bulletins from services attended by Rev. Little Wyman.

Materials relating to the work of Gary Huber

Common Cathedral community writings and reflections

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Further sermons and liturgical writings by Deborah Little Wyman are in Series I, Program Documentation.

title.part: Home Care for the Dying: An Authoritative, Reassuring Guide to Physical and Emotional Care

did.unittitle: "Theology of the Poor" in Handbook of U.S. Theologies of Liberation

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An account of the origins and ministry of Common Cathedral.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Containing "Q: Should we give money to panhandlers?," "Found on the Street," and "Ministry on the Streets: Come and See."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Containing articles and sermons by others gathered by Deborah Little Wyman.

did.unittitle: Funeral and memorial services: sermons and liturgies
Ordination and installation: sermons and liturgies
LGBTQ+ and AIDS: sermons and liturgies of healing, remembrance, prayer, and pride
Topical gathered materials: domestic violence and addiction
